okf_json.overview: The JSON Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) files, based on the JSON specifications at json.org. It extracts translatable string values from JSON key-value pairs and supports configurable extraction rules, inline code detection, s

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling. When enabled, specified regular expressions are applied to extracted text and any matches are converted to inline codes. Cannot be used together with the sub-filter
notes: Note: This option cannot be used together with the sub-filtering option.

extraction.description: Control what content is extracted for translation. Provides options to extract all key-value pairs or use regex-based extraction rules, include isolated strings from arrays, exclude specific keys, and

extraction.extractAll.description: When true, extracts all strings that have an associated key. If exceptions are defined, strings whose keys match the exception expression are not extracted. When false (Do not extract key/string pairs
notes: If extraction rules (pathRules) are specified (>= M39), they override this setting and the exception-based rules.

extraction.pathRules.description: Regex matching keys whose values are extracted. If specified, this overrides the extractAll setting and the exception-based extraction rules. Applied to key names (or full key paths if useFullPath is
notes: If specified, these will override the corresponding extractAll/exception rules above.

extraction.includeIsolatedStrings.description: Extract string values that are not associated directly to a key, such as strings in arrays. When enabled, standalone string values in JSON arrays are extracted as translatable content.

extraction.excludeKeys.description: A comma-separated list of keys (or a regular expression) that should be excluded from or included in extraction, depending on the extractAll setting. When extractAll is true, keys matching this expres

limitations: Comments within a JSON string are parsed as part of the string content, not as comments. A configured subfilter will then process these as true comments (they will become part of the skeleton or whatever the filter is configured to do).
processingNotes: JSON files are normally in Unicode encoding, but the filter supports any encoding. If a Unicode BOM is present, the corresponding encoding is used. Otherwise, if a charset declaration exists in the first 1000 characters, that encoding is used (with BOM taking precedence if both exist and conflict). Otherwise, the default encoding is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was UTF-8 and had a BOM. If converting from another encoding to UTF-8, no BOM is written.
The type of line-breaks in the output matches the original input.

examples.title: Basic JSON extraction
examples.description: A simple JSON file showing which string values are extracted as translatable content (highlighted with span tags in wiki).

examples.title: Using idRules to extract resname from sibling key
examples.description: When JSON contains arrays of objects with a 'key' field alongside a 'text' field, using idRules with regex 'key' causes the value of the 'key' field to become the resname of the text unit.
examples.output:
<trans-unit id="tu1" resname="datePicker_marchMonth" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en-US">March</source>
</trans-unit>
<trans-unit id="tu2" resname="datePicker_aprilMonth" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en-US">April</source>
</trans-unit>

examples.title: FPRM extraction rules example
examples.description: Example regex rules applied to key names for extraction, notes, IDs, and metadata. Shows how different rule types target different key paths under /widgets/.
examples.output: Keys matching /widgets/body.* are extracted; /widgets/name.* values become notes; /widgets/id.* values become resnames; /widgets/image.* values become metadata.

okf_html.overview: The HTML Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for HTML and XHTML documents. It supports both well-formed XHTML and non-well-formed HTML through configurable parsing modes. The filter uses YAML-based configuration files to define element and attribute extraction rules, c

parser.description: Parser behavior settings controlling how the HTML input is parsed.

parser.assumeWellformed.description: When set to true, the parser assumes the input HTML is well-formed XML, which enables faster parsing but may fail on non-conforming HTML. When false (default), the parser uses a more lenient HTML pars
notes: The predefined okf_html-wellFormed configuration sets this to true and includes GROUP rules for structural elements like table, ul, ol, dl, etc.
If structural groupings are needed, use the well-formed configuration, but all structural tags mapped to groups must be properly closed.

parser.preserveWhitespace.description: When true, preserves original whitespace in all extracted text globally. When false (default), whitespace is normalized except inside elements that have the PRESERVE_WHITESPACE rule type (e.g., <pre>)

skipEncodingDeclaration.description: Controls whether the filter skips processing of encoding declarations in the HTML document. When false (default), encoding declarations are processed normally.

attributes.description: Global attribute extraction rules that apply across multiple elements. Each key is a lowercase attribute name, and the value defines how that attribute should be handled across the document. Rules can
notes: All attribute names must be in lowercase in the configuration file, regardless of their casing in the document.
Prefixed attributes (e.g., xml:lang) must be enclosed in single quotes in the YAML configuration.

elements.description: Element extraction rules that map HTML element names to their processing behavior. Each key is a lowercase element name, and the value defines how the filter treats that element and its attributes. El
notes: All element names must be in lowercase in the configuration file.
Elements with GROUP rule type must be well-formed (have both start and end tags) when assumeWellformed is true, otherwise the filter returns an error.

limitations: Content of <style> and <script> elements is not extracted.
Tags from server-side scripts such as PHP, ASPX, JSP, etc. are not formally supported and will be treated as non-translatable.
processingNotes: Encoding detection: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise the default encoding specified in filter options is used.
UTF-8 BOM handling: If both input and output are UTF-8, a BOM is written only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8 but output is UTF-8, no BOM is written.
If the input file has no declared encoding, the filter tries to add a <meta> tag (HTML) or <meta /> tag (XHTML) in output, but only if there is a <head> element in the file.

examples.title: Inline code finder for variables
examples.description: Using the code finder to protect variable placeholders like VAR1 from modification during translation
examples.output:
In

Number of files = VAR1

, 'VAR1' is treated as an inline code placeholder

examples.title: Inline code finder alternate YAML syntax
examples.description: Using YAML block scalar syntax for more readable code finder rules
examples.output: Same result as escaped syntax — VAR1 is detected as inline code

examples.title: Simple translatable attributes
examples.description: Specifying attributes that should always be translatable on an element, using a simple list
examples.output: The accesskey, area, and alt attributes of <area> elements are extracted as translatable

okf_xliff.overview: The XLIFF Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for XLIFF 1.2 (XML Localisation Interchange File Format) documents. XLIFF is an OASIS Standard that defines a file format for transporting translatable text and localization-related information across a chain of translation

extraction.preserveSpaceByDefault.description: When enabled, whitespace is preserved by default in source and target content. When disabled, if a <trans-unit> element has xml:space="preserve", white spaces inside source and target are left as-is;

extraction.includeExtensions.description: Set this option to include non-standard information (e.g. match types) in added <alt-trans> elements. When enabled, extra information beyond the XLIFF standard is preserved in alt-trans output.

extraction.includeIts.description: When enabled, ITS and ITSXLF annotations are included during extraction. These annotations carry localization metadata such as translate, terminology, and localization note information.

extraction.inlineCdata.description: When enabled, CDATA sections encountered in source/target content are treated as inline codes rather than as text content.

extraction.subAsTextUnit.description: When enabled, <sub> elements found inside <bpt>, <ept>, <ph>, and <it> elements are processed as separate text units instead of being included in the code of the parent inline element.

extraction.ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Set this option to ignore any segmentation information contained in the input XLIFF. When this option is set, all segmented content is reduced to new unsegmented content when extracted. Note that any
notes: Any <alt-trans> data attached to a given segment is also lost when this option is enabled.

limitations: The content of the <sub> element is currently not supported as text. Any element found inside <bpt>, <ept>, <ph>, and <it> (including <sub>) is included in the code of the parent inline element. A warning is generated when a <sub> element is detected.

The special marker <mrk mtype='protected'> is supported by converting content into an inline code. If <mrk mtype='x-its-translate-yes'> is used within such a marker, it is not supported and its content is placed into the original data part of the inline code.

The pre-defined configuration okf_xliff-sdl provides additional support for SDLXLIFF files. SDL properties (locked, conf, origin) are stored in the TextContainer of the target rather than in each segment; if there are several segments, the values are those of the last segment. From M35 on, segment-level properties are also stored with correct values but are read-only.
processingNotes: If the document has an encoding declaration it is used; otherwise UTF-8 is used as the default encoding regardless of any specified default.
If input and output encoding are both UTF-8, a BOM is used in output only if one was detected in the input. If input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used in the UTF-8 output.
Line-break type in the output matches the original input.

examples.title: Alt-trans mapping
examples.description: How <alt-trans> elements are mapped to Okapi AltTranslationsAnnotation. The match-quality attribute is used as score (integer or percentage), origin attribute maps to annotation origin (defaults to 'S

examples.title: Segmentation in seg-source
examples.description: How <seg-source> segmentation is handled. The segmentation is carried over only if the content of <seg-source> without markers matches <source>. If no explicit <mrk mtype='seg'> elements exist, the wh
examples.input:
<trans-unit id="1">
  <source>First sentence. Second sentence.</source>
  <seg-source>
    <mrk mtype="seg" mid="1">First sentence.</mrk>
    <mrk mtype="seg" mid="2">Second sentence.</mrk>
  </seg-source>
</trans-unit>
examples.output: Two segments extracted: 'First sentence.' and 'Second sentence.'

examples.title: Output segmentation types
examples.description: The four output segmentation modes control how segmentation is represented in output: (0) preserve input segmentation, (1) always add seg-source, (2) never segment/remove all seg-source, (3) segment o

okf_properties.overview: The Properties Filter is an Okapi component that implements the IFilter interface for Java properties files (.properties, .lang). The implementation is based on the specification found in the Java java.util.Properties class documentation, with support for additional features for compatibility with o

extraction.useKeyCondition.description: Set this option to extract items based on their keys. When enabled, you specify a regular expression pattern; if the key matches the pattern, the item is extracted or not depending on the extractOnlyM
notes: Note: directives have precedence over key condition.

extraction.extractOnlyMatchingKey.description: When true, only items with keys matching the keyCondition pattern are extracted. When false, items with keys matching the pattern are excluded from extraction (i.e., do not extract matching items).

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this setting to have any effect

extraction.keyCondition.description: A regular expression pattern to test against the key of each entry. For example, '.*text.*' matches any key containing the word 'text'. The pattern must be a valid Java regular expression.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this pattern to be applied

extraction.localizeOutside.description: Set this option to extract any translatable item that is not within the scope of a localization directive. Selecting to extract or not outside localization directives allows you to mark up fewer parts

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this option to be enabled

extraction.extraComments.description: Set this option to take into account other comment styles in addition to the strict Java comments (single-line starting with '#' or '!'). When this option is set, the filter also recognizes single-lin

notes.commentsAreNotes.description: Set this option to include the comments before each entry as a note property on the text unit of the corresponding entry. All comment lines are grouped into a single note.

processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (e.g. UTF-8, UTF-16) is used; otherwise the default encoding specified in the filter options is used.
If the output encoding is UTF-8 and the input was also UTF-8, a BOM is used in the output only if one was detected in the input. If the input was not UTF-8, no BOM is used in the output.
The type of line-breaks in the output is the same as the one of the original input.

examples.title: Basic properties extraction
examples.description: Simple properties file with comments. Both key=value and key:value separators are supported.
examples.input:
# Example of Java properties

labelOK= OK
msgBadFile: Invalid input file
examples.output: Two text units extracted: 'OK' (name=labelOK) and 'Invalid input file' (name=msgBadFile)

examples.title: Key condition filtering
examples.description: Using key condition with pattern '.*text.*' and extractOnlyMatchingKey=true to extract only entries whose keys contain 'text'.
examples.input:
key1 = Text for key1
text.err1 = Text for text.err1
menu_text_file = Text for menu_text_file
examples.output: Only 'Text for text.err1' (key=text.err1) and 'Text for menu_text_file' (key=menu_text_file) are extracted. 'key1' does not match the pattern and is skipped.

okf_po.overview: The PO Filter implements the IFilter interface for Gettext PO (Portable Object) resource files and POT (PO templates). It is based on the PO specifications from the GNU gettext manual and supports bilingual and monolingual processing modes, plural forms, domains, and various PO comment types. The fi

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling. When enabled, the specified regular expressions are applied to extracted text items, and any match is converted to an inline code. The default expression matches pr
notes: The default regex pattern covers three categories: printf format specifiers like %d/%s/%f, escape sequences like \n/\r/\t, and curly-brace placeholders like {1}.

mode.description: Processing mode settings that control how the PO file is interpreted — either as a bilingual file (source in msgid, translation in msgstr) or as a monolingual file (real ID in msgid, text in msgstr).

bilingualMode.description: When enabled, the input document is processed as a bilingual file where the msgid entry is the source text and the msgstr entry is the translation. This is the normal standard way PO files are process

protectApproved.description: When enabled, all entries that are not empty and not fuzzy are set with a non-translatable flag, protecting approved translations from changes. The fuzzy flag in PO files is indicated by '#, fuzzy' an

wrapContent.description: Controls whether long content lines in the output are wrapped. When enabled, long msgid and msgstr lines will be broken across multiple quoted strings, following PO file conventions.

identification.description: ID and naming settings controlling how text unit identifiers are generated and how context information is handled.

processingNotes: Encoding detection follows a priority chain: (1) Unicode BOM if present, (2) charset declaration in header entry within first 1000 characters, (3) default encoding from filter options. If both BOM and header charset exist and conflict, a warning is generated and the BOM encoding takes precedence.
If the header charset value is literally 'charset' (case insensitive), the file is treated as a template with no declared encoding, and the auto-detected or default encoding is used.

Output encoding: if the file has a header charset declaration, it is automatically updated to reflect the output encoding. For UTF-8 output, a BOM is only included if the input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM.

examples.title: Basic PO file structure
examples.description: A simple PO file with header entry (empty msgid) containing encoding and plural information, followed by translatable entries including a plural form entry.
examples.input:
# PO file for myApp

msgid ""
msgstr ""
"Project-Id-Version: myApp 1.0.0\n"
"Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\n"
"Plural-Forms: nplurals=2; plural=(n != 1);\n"

msgid "diverging after version %d of %s"
msgstr ""

msgid "You have selected %d file for deletion"
msgid_plural "You have selected %

examples.title: Plural forms extraction to XLIFF
examples.description: Shows how plural form entries (msgid/msgid_plural/msgstr[N]) are extracted as a group with type 'x-gettext-plurals'. Each plural form gets a separate trans-unit with an indexed resource name (e.g., P3

examples.title: Domain support
examples.description: Shows how domain directives are represented as groups with type 'x-gettext-domain' and the domain name as the resource name.
examples.input:
domain TheDomain1
msgid "Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'"
msgstr "Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'"
examples.output:
<group resname="TheDomain1" restype="x-gettext-domain">
  <trans-unit id="1" resname="N9D1999AB">
    <source>Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'</source>
    <target>Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'</target>
  </trans-unit>
</group>

batch-translation.overview: This step creates a translation memory from text units extracted from a raw document, using an external tool to provide the translation. It extracts content via the document's associated filter, creates a temporary HTML document with source segments, invokes an external translation tool via a user-d

command.description: The command line used to invoke the external translation tool. The command must take the temporary HTML document named ${inputPath} and generate an output document in the same format named ${outputPat
notes: The external tool must create the translated HTML output at the path corresponding to ${outputPath} or ${outputURI}.

blockSize.description: The maximum number of text units that should be passed at the same time to the external tool. This allows processing of very large input documents even with external tools that can only handle small d

origin.description: An optional string that identifies the origin of the translation. The given string is output as a property of the translated entry named 'Origin'. For example, in TMX output it will be generated as <p

markAsMT.description: When enabled, marks the TM entries generated as the result of machine translation. For example, when this option is set, the creationId attribute of the target in the generated output is set to 'MT!'.

segment.description: When enabled, segments the extracted text units using SRX rules before sending them to the temporary HTML document. If set, each paragraph of the HTML document will be a sentence. If not set, each par
notes: Note: Only entries processed by the external tool are placed in the TMX output. Entries that already exist in the TM being populated or in the existing TM are not copied into the TMX output.

srxPath.description: Full path to the segmentation rules file in SRX format that should be used to segment the text units. You can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir} in the path, as well as any of the locale

processingNotes: The step extracts content from the raw document using its associated filter and places it in a temporary HTML document where each paragraph corresponds to a source segment (or source text unit if not segmented).
Each input document can be processed using one or more temporary HTML files based on the block size, allowing tools with size limitations to translate very large documents.
Entries added to the Pensieve TM are indexed at the end of each input document, so downstream steps can only access entries generated from previous documents.

examples.title: Apertium translation on Linux
examples.description: Uses the open-source Apertium machine translation program under Linux to translate the temporary HTML document.

examples.title: ProMT translation on Windows
examples.description: Uses the commercial ProMT application under Windows to translate the temporary HTML document.

cleanup.overview: This step cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, and whitespace ready for further processing. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. By default, all whitespace is normalized before any further processing is performed — all multiple space, tab, etc. characters

normalizeQuotes.description: When enabled, replaces all quotation marks with straight double quotes (") and all apostrophes with single straight quotes ('). This normalization is applied to both source and target text.

checkCharacters.description: When enabled, detects and removes text units that contain common corrupt character strings. This helps clean data that may have encoding issues or unexpected characters.

matchRegexExpressions.description: Mark segments matching default regular expressions for removal. This option is not currently used.
notes: This option is not currently used.

matchUserRegex.description: When enabled, removes text units that contain text matching the user-defined regular expression. This allows custom pattern-based filtering of segments.

pruneTextUnit.description: When enabled, removes text units that have been marked for removal or have no target text. This cleans up segments flagged by other cleanup operations or those lacking translations.

normalizePunctuations.description: When enabled, standardizes spaces before and after certain punctuation marks to English writing style. This normalizes punctuation spacing conventions.

limitations: Does not work with Asian or bi-directional languages.
processingNotes: By default, all whitespace is normalized before any further processing is performed; all multiple space, tab, etc. characters are replaced with a single instance.
The step processes both source and target content of text units.
Text units marked for removal by regex matching or corrupt character detection are removed during the pruning phase if pruneTextUnit is enabled.

character-count.overview: This step performs character counts on the different parts of a set of documents. It takes filter events and sends filter events. The character counts are saved as annotations that can be used in other steps, such as the Scoping Report Step which can generate a report from the counts. The character

countInBatch.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per batch. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregate

countInBatchItems.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per batch item. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggr

countInDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per document. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggreg

countInSubDocuments.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per sub-document. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional ag

countInGroups.description: Set this option to get an annotation of the character count per group. A character count annotation is always set on each text unit regardless of this setting; this option adds an additional aggregate

bufferSize.description: Size of text buffer used for character counting. A value of 0 indicates the default buffer size.

processingNotes: Character count annotations are always set on each text unit, regardless of the aggregation-level options selected.
The character count follows the GMX-V 2.0 standard (http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html).
Count annotations can be consumed by downstream steps such as the Scoping Report Step.

search-and-replace.overview: This step performs search and replace actions on either the text units or the full content of input documents. It can take as input either a raw document (operating on the whole file) or filter events (operating on text unit content). Patterns are processed in the order they are declared, and both p

regEx.description: Enables regular expression mode for all search and replace patterns. When enabled, search patterns are interpreted as Java regular expressions (java.util.regex.Pattern), and replacement strings suppor
notes: The placeholders for search group references in replacement strings are "$1", "$2", etc. See Matcher.replaceAll() documentation for full details.
In all cases (regex on or off), you must use \\ to represent a literal backslash. For any sequence \C where C is not a special case, the result is the literal character itself.

dotAll.description: Changes the meaning of the period character '.' so that it matches every character instead of every character except \n (line-feed). This is the DOTALL flag in Java regex. Applies to all expressions,

ignoreCase.description: Ignores case differences when matching patterns. For example, 'bear' matches 'Bearcat', 'BEARCAT', and 'bearcat'. This is the CASE_INSENSITIVE flag in Java regex. Applies to all expressions, not just

multiLine.description: Changes the meaning of '^' and '$' so that they match at the beginning and end of any line, not just the beginning and end of the whole string. This is the MULTILINE flag in Java regex. Applies to all

target.description: When processing text units (filter events input), performs the search and replace on the target content. Enabled by default. This option is ignored if the step is processing a raw document.
notes: When working on the target content, you may have to use the Create Target Step before this step to make sure there is content in the target.

source.description: When processing text units (filter events input), performs the search and replace on the source content. Not enabled by default. This option is ignored if the step is processing a raw document.

limitations: When working with a raw document as input, the whole content of the document is loaded in memory. This may result in problems with very large documents.
The replacements from the external tab-delimited file (replacementsPath) are limited to literal searches only (non-regex, true substring matching).
processingNotes: If the step receives filter events, search and replace operates on text unit content and sends updated filter events. If it receives a raw document, it operates on the whole file content and sends an updated raw document.
When processing a raw document, the file must be in a text-based format — the document is seen exactly as it would be in a text editor with no conversion of escaped characters.
Patterns are processed in the order they are declared in the list. Longer patterns should be placed first if there is a chance of overlapping matches.

examples.title: Plain text search and replace
examples.description: When regular expressions are OFF, patterns support literal text with escape sequences. Search for a literal string and replace with another. Special sequences \n, \r, \t, \\, and \uHHHH are supported
examples.output: All occurrences of 'old-term' are replaced with 'new-term' in the target content of each text unit.

examples.title: Regex search with group references
examples.description: When regular expressions are ON, search patterns use Java regex syntax and replacements can reference captured groups using $1, $2, etc. The dotAll, multiLine, and ignoreCase flags apply globally to a
examples.output: Email-like patterns are reformatted, e.g., 'user@example.com' becomes '[user at example.com]'.

examples.title: External replacements file
examples.description: A tab-delimited UTF-8 file can provide additional literal replacements processed after the main pattern list. Each line has two columns: search string (tab) replacement string.
examples.output: After all table patterns are applied, literal substring replacements from the file are applied in order.

segmentation.overview: The Segmentation step segments the content of extracted text units into smaller parts, usually corresponding to sentences, using SRX 2.0 (Segmentation Rules eXchange) rules. It takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output. Text units flagged as non-translatable, text units with no

segmentSource.description: When enabled, segments the source text of the text units using the specified SRX rules document. The SRX document path can use the variables ${rootDir} and ${inputRootDir}.
notes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are not segmented.
Text units with no content are not segmented.

segmentTarget.description: When enabled, segments the existing target text of the text units using the specified SRX rules document, if there is text for the target locales being processed. Only the locales currently being proc
notes: Only target locales being processed are affected, even if the document contains additional locales.

renumberCodes.description: When enabled, changes the IDs of inline codes in each resulting segment so they all start at '1'. This can be useful when working with translation memories where each segment restarts the IDs of its i
notes: Warning: This option cannot work with documents where the ID values are not numeric, or not sequential.
Using this option when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with some formats that are segment-based like TTX.

checkSegments.description: When enabled, verifies that if there is a target available, all source segments have a corresponding target segment in the target content. This also verifies that both source and target have the same
notes: This verification does not ensure that the content of a target segment is the translation of its corresponding source text. It only matches their segment ID.

trimSrcLeadingWS.description: Controls trimming of leading whitespace from source segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

trimSrcTrailingWS.description: Controls trimming of trailing whitespace from source segments. A value of -1 means no trimming (default behavior).

limitations: Strict implementation of SRX syntax in Java is not possible. See the 'SRX and Java' page for details on the limitations.
Using the 'Renumber code IDs' option when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with some segment-based formats like TTX.
The renumber codes option cannot work with documents where inline code ID values are not numeric or not sequential.
processingNotes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are not segmented.
Text units with no content are not segmented.
Text units that are already segmented are not re-segmented unless the 'overwriteSegmentation' or 'deepenSegmentation' option is set.

ruleTypes.description: Rule types control how elements and attributes are handled during extraction. Each element or attribute rule must have a ruleTypes array.

conditions.description: Conditions are triples [attributeName, operator, value] that make extraction rules conditional on an attribute's value.
